author: Macmill-340
text:
WHAT IS PYTHON?
Python is a programming language — a way of giving precise instructions to a computer so it can perform tasks on your behalf. Unlike human language, which is flexible and context-dependent, a programming language is exact. The computer does exactly what you write, nothing more, nothing less. This is both its power and its challenge.

Python was created by a Dutch programmer named Guido van Rossum. He started working on it in the late 1980s and released the first version in 1991. The name has nothing to do with the snake. Van Rossum was a fan of the British comedy group Monty Python, and he wanted a name that was short, memorable, and a little bit fun. The snake imagery came later, adopted by the community and now inseparable from the language's identity.

WHY PYTHON?
Python was designed with one idea at the centre: code should be readable. A Python program should look roughly like structured English — clear enough that someone who has never seen it before can follow the logic without a manual. This makes Python one of the most approachable first languages to learn, but it also makes it a language that professionals return to and stay with.

It is not a toy language. Python is one of the most widely used programming languages in the world, and it is the dominant language in several of the most active areas of technology today.

WHERE IS PYTHON USED?
Web development — frameworks like Django and Flask power large parts of the internet, from news sites to government platforms.

Data analysis and science — Python is the standard tool for working with data. Libraries like pandas and NumPy allow researchers, analysts, and engineers to process, explore, and visualise datasets that would be impossible to handle manually.

Artificial intelligence and machine learning — nearly all modern AI research and production systems are built on Python. If you have used a recommendation system, a language model, or an image classifier, Python was almost certainly involved in building it.

Automation and scripting — Python is widely used to automate repetitive tasks: renaming files in bulk, scraping data from websites, sending automated emails, or testing software.

Scientific computing — physicists, biologists, economists, and engineers use Python to run simulations, model systems, and analyse experimental results.

Game development, cybersecurity, finance, robotics — Python appears across virtually every technical domain.

WHAT YOU ARE LEARNING
The lessons ahead cover the fundamentals: how to store values, how to display output, how to make decisions in code, how to repeat actions, and how to organise data. These concepts appear in every programming language. Learning them well in Python means you are also building a mental model that transfers.

You do not need any previous programming experience. You do not need to be good at mathematics. What you need is patience and a willingness to read error messages instead of ignoring them — because every error is telling you something specific.

Press Enter to begin with Lesson 1.
